Q1.root_cause: Floating-point arithmetic order causing rounding differences in aggregations. Q1 is already highly optimized with parallel scan and thread-local aggregation. Performance is excellent (128ms for 60M rows on SF10), already 2x faster than DuckDB's best time. The bottleneck is correctness, not performance.

Q3.root_cause: CRITICAL: Wrong results - 3 out of 10 rows have completely different orderkeys, revenues, and dates. This indicates a fundamental logic bug in the join or aggregation. Additionally, the join pipeline is single-threaded while Q1/Q6 use parallel scans. The hash join probe (60M lineitem rows) is sequential, causing 3.6s runtime vs target <100ms. Join ordering is correct (customer->orders->lineitem) but execution lacks parallelism.

Q6.root_cause: Q6 is perfect: correct results, 47ms runtime for 60M rows on SF10 (2.5x faster than DuckDB). Parallel scan with predicate pushdown is working optimally. No optimization needed.

analysis.overall: Iteration 1 has one critical correctness bug (Q3) that MUST be fixed before performance optimization. Q1 has minor floating-point precision differences (semantically correct). Q6 is perfect. Performance analysis: Q3 dominates total time (93%) and is 35-40x slower than DuckDB/target. Root cause: single-threaded join probe over 60M rows + potential correctness bug in aggregation logic. Q1 and Q6 are already highly optimized and outperform DuckDB.

critical_fixes.issue: Incorrect query results - wrong orderkeys and revenues in output
critical_fixes.description: Q3 produces 3 out of 10 rows with completely different values (orderkeys like 4791171, 47009378 vs expected). Likely causes: (1) Aggregation key mismatch - the Q3Key struct uses (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) but should verify these come from the correct join side. (2) Hash collision in Q3KeyHash using simple XOR - consider using better hash function. (3) Hash table lookup failure - customer_filter.find() returns .end() incorrectly. Debug by: (1) Print intermediate join cardinalities (customer filter output, orders filter output, final aggregates count). (2) Verify join key types match (all int32_t). (3) Check that o_orderdate/o_shippriority come from the orders_ht payload, not from lineitem columns. (4) Add assertions that aggregation keys are unique per order.

critical_fixes.issue: Minor floating-point precision differences in aggregates
critical_fixes.fix: Use Kahan summation for stable aggregation
critical_fixes.description: Q1 shows differences in 9th-10th decimal place for sum_charge and sum_disc_price. This is due to parallel aggregation changing the order of floating-point additions. Fix: Implement compensated (Kahan) summation in Q1Agg struct to maintain precision across thread boundaries. Add a 'compensation' field to track accumulated rounding errors. Alternative: Sort rows before aggregation to ensure deterministic order (but this hurts performance). Given this is semantically correct, this fix has lower priority than Q3 correctness.

performance_optimizations.description: Q3's lineitem probe (60M rows) is currently single-threaded (hash_join_probe_filtered in operators/hash_join.h line 146). This is the #1 performance bottleneck. Parallelize the probe phase: Split lineitem into chunks (morsel size ~100K rows) and process in parallel threads. Each thread independently: (1) filters l_shipdate > cutoff, (2) probes orders_ht, (3) aggregates into thread-local hash table. After all threads complete, merge thread-local aggregates into global map. Implementation: Copy parallel_scan pattern from Q1/Q6 queries. Replace lines 149-155 in q3.cpp with parallel_scan wrapper that creates per-thread std::unordered_map<Q3Key, double> and merges at the end. Expected impact: 60M rows / 64 threads = ~1M rows per thread, should achieve 8-16x speedup. Target time: 3634ms / 12 = ~300ms (conservative estimate accounting for merge overhead).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 12-20x speedup (3634ms -> 180-300ms). Q3 becomes dominated by hash table build rather than probe. This brings Q3 into competitive range with DuckDB (94ms best time).

performance_optimizations.description: Q3 sequentially mmaps 10 columns across 3 tables but doesn't provide prefetch hints to the kernel. On HDD storage (noted in storage_design.toon), this causes blocking I/O waits. Fix: In q3.cpp after each mmap_column call, immediately issue madvise(ptr, size, MADV_WILLNEED) to trigger asynchronous prefetch. The kernel will start reading pages in the background while the query continues. Also add madvise(ptr, size, MADV_SEQUENTIAL) for columnar scans to optimize page-ahead behavior. Impact: Reduces I/O wait time by overlapping computation with I/O. Expected 20-30% improvement on HDD, minimal on SSD. Specific files: customer columns (lines 81-82), orders columns (lines 100-103), lineitem columns (lines 130-133) in q3.cpp.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-30% faster on HDD (reduce from 3.6s to ~2.5-2.9s). Combined with Priority 1, target 150-250ms.

performance_optimizations.description: Q3 uses std::unordered_map<Q3Key, double> for aggregation (line 138 in q3.cpp). std::unordered_map has 100-200% memory overhead (linked list chaining) and poor cache locality. Q3 aggregates ~100K-500K groups (estimated in workload_analysis), causing frequent cache misses. Replace with a compact hash table: Use Robin Hood hashing or Google's dense_hash_map with open addressing. Store entries contiguously in a single array. Pre-size to expected group count: hash_table.reserve(500000). This reduces memory from ~40MB (std::unordered_map) to ~16MB (compact), improving L3 cache hit rate. Implement custom hash table in operators/compact_hash.h or use Abseil's flat_hash_map. Expected: 15-25% faster aggregation due to better cache behavior and fewer allocations.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25% speedup in aggregation phase (after fixing correctness). Combined with priorities 1-2, target Q3 time: 120-200ms.

performance_optimizations.description: Q3 filters 60M lineitem rows by l_shipdate > cutoff (line 141 in q3.cpp). This is a scalar comparison in a hot loop. The CPU supports AVX-512 (detected: avx512f, avx512bw in lscpu). Vectorize the filter: Use AVX-512 to compare 16 int32_t dates simultaneously (_mm512_cmpgt_epi32_mask), producing a 16-bit mask. Process lineitem in batches of 16 rows, generate a selection bitmap, then probe hash table only for matching rows. This reduces branch mispredictions (filtering is ~57% selective per workload_analysis) and increases instruction throughput. Implementation: Create operators/simd_filter.h with avx512_date_filter(int32_t* dates, int32_t cutoff, size_t count) -> std::vector<size_t> indices. Apply before hash join probe. Expected: 2-3x faster filtering, ~20-30% overall speedup on Q3 probe phase.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-30% speedup on lineitem scan (after parallelization). Combines multiplicatively with Priority 1. Target: 90-150ms for Q3.

performance_optimizations.description: Q1 computes disc_price = price * (1 - disc) and charge = disc_price * (1 + tax) for every row (lines 89-94 in q1.cpp). These are scalar floating-point operations. Vectorize with AVX-512: load 8 double values at a time for price/disc/tax, compute products in parallel using _mm512_mul_pd and _mm512_fmadd_pd (fused multiply-add). Accumulate sums in SIMD registers using horizontal reduction at the end. This reduces arithmetic latency and better utilizes the CPU's FMA units. Expected: 2-3x speedup on arithmetic, ~40-50% overall improvement. Target: 128ms -> 65-90ms. This would make Q1 3-4x faster than DuckDB's best time (83ms), but may be unnecessary given Q1 is already very fast.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 40-50% speedup (128ms -> 65-90ms). Diminishing returns - Q1 is already 2x faster than DuckDB.

performance_optimizations.description: Q3 currently aggregates ALL matching groups into a hash table (100K-500K groups estimated), converts to vector, runs top_k_selection on full results (lines 164-180 in q3.cpp). This wastes memory and CPU on groups that will never make the top 10. Optimization: During the aggregation phase, maintain a min-heap of size 10 alongside the hash table. After aggregating each group, check if its revenue exceeds the heap's minimum (heap.top()). If yes, evict the minimum and insert the new group. At the end, the heap contains the exact top 10 results. This avoids allocating/sorting the full result vector (100K entries). Expected: 30-50% faster final aggregation/sorting phase, 5-10% overall Q3 improvement. Most beneficial when combined with parallel probing (Priority 1).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% overall Q3 speedup by eliminating large vector allocation and sort. Target: contribute to reaching 80-120ms.

performance_optimizations.description: All queries call storage::mmap_column sequentially for each column (Q3 mmaps 10 columns one by one). On HDD, this serializes I/O. Optimization: Batch all column mmap calls and issue them in parallel threads, each with MAP_POPULATE flag to trigger immediate page fault resolution. The kernel will parallelize reads across columns. Implementation: Add storage::mmap_columns_parallel(vector<string> paths) that spawns threads, mmaps with MAP_POPULATE, and returns vector of pointers. Use in q1.cpp (lines 60-67), q3.cpp (lines 81-82, 100-103, 130-133), q6.cpp (lines 22-26). Expected: 2-3x faster column loading on HDD (reduce from ~50-100ms to ~20-30ms per query). Minimal impact on query time but improves user-perceived latency.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-3x faster mmap initialization (50-100ms -> 20-30ms per query). Reduces 'time to first byte' latency, minimal impact on total query time.

summary: Iteration 1 has one CRITICAL correctness bug in Q3 that must be fixed immediately before performance optimization. Once fixed, the #1 performance bottleneck is Q3's single-threaded hash join probe over 60M lineitem rows. Parallelizing the probe (Priority 1) + prefetching (Priority 2) + compact hash tables (Priority 3) should reduce Q3 from 3634ms to 120-200ms (18-30x speedup), making it competitive with DuckDB (94ms). Additional SIMD optimizations (Priority 4-5) can push Q3 below 100ms and Q1 below 70ms. Q6 requires no changes (already optimal at 47ms). Hardware: 64-core Xeon with AVX-512 is severely underutilized by single-threaded joins. No storage layout changes needed - columnar format with mmap is working well. Focus: Fix Q3 correctness, then parallelize join probe.
